steps.title: Extract the key research finding
steps.content: The study found that for a recording both a musician and a nonmusician identified as a favorite, the musician's optimal listening volume was higher than the nonmusician's. Applied to "Sad but True": the musician's optimal is 85.5 decibels, so the nonmusician's optimal must be below 85.5 decibels. Listening at 85.5 decibels puts the nonmusician above their optimal volume.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the match
steps.content:
Work through each option:
  • Choice A — 'enjoy listening to the nature sounds at 85.5 decibels more than listening to "Sad but True" at 85.5 decibels.': The study compares musician vs. nonmusician optimal volumes for the same recording, not nature sounds vs. music for the same listener. ✗
  • Choice B — 'prefer listening to other music at 85.5 decibels over listening to "Sad but True" at that volume.': No cross-genre comparison is made; the finding is about optimal volume for a shared favorite recording. ✗
  • Choice C — 'not find it enjoyable to listen to any recordings at a volume as low as 85.5 decibels.': Overstates the finding; the nonmusician has a lower optimal, but 85.5 decibels is not claimed to be unenjoyable for all recordings. ✗
  • Choice D — 'find listening to "Sad but True" at 85.5 decibels less enjoyable than listening to it at a lower volume.': Since the nonmusician's optimal is below 85.5 decibels, listening above that optimal is less enjoyable than listening at it. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
